//: Correctness rules only — deliberately NOT `extends: stylelint-config-standard`.
That preset is largely a formatter: it would demand its own indentation, quoting
and case conventions across the whole styles/ tree and bury the two or three
findings that actually matter under a thousand cosmetic ones. The house style
here is tabs + one-liners for short rules, and it is deliberate.
What is switched on below is the set that can catch a real bug: a typo'd
property or pseudo-class, a broken hex, the same property twice in one block,
an empty block, a duplicate selector. Plus the project's own invariants from
CLAUDE.md — no stray !important, and no colour literal outside the palettes.
audit.py stays: it checks things stylelint cannot see (var() defined-vs-used
across files, declarations shadowed within a cascade layer).
TWO formatting rules are the deliberate exception, and they are @stylistic/* because
stylelint 16 removed every stylistic rule from core. The objection above is to the
PRESET, not to the idea: a preset imposes a hundred opinions nobody chose, while these
two each close a measured drift the house style already had a majority on — quotes were
309 double vs 47 single in attribute selectors, leading zeros 97 bare vs 5. Picking the
majority cost 77 autofixed characters and changed the built cascade.css by 0 bytes
(116548 both ways; the awk squeeze is string-aware and eats either quote identically).
string-quotes leaves a string that CONTAINS the preferred quote alone rather than escape
it — which is why the four <svg xmlns="…"> data URIs in 03-palettes.css stay single.
That is the rule's own definition, not an exemption we wrote.
What still CANNOT be gated, so it is on review alone: attribute-quote PRESENCE
(input[type=file]) and `0px` — stylelint 16 removed selector-attribute-quotes and
length-zero-no-unit and @stylistic never ported either.
The second block of rules below is the same argument one layer deeper: all of them
are correctness, none is formatting, and every one found ZERO violations in the
current tree — so they cost nothing today and catch the next mistake for free.
declaration-property-value-no-unknown is the one that matters most: it is the only
check that reads a declaration's VALUE against the property's grammar, i.e. the only
thing that can catch a value which is invalid at computed-value time and therefore
vanishes in SILENCE — the exact failure mode this project tore the --*-rgb component
bridges out for. custom-property-no-missing-var-function catches `color: --fs-accent`
(a bare custom-property name where var() was meant), which parses and does nothing.
The only files allowed an !important, and each for a documented reason.
theme/90-responsive + pages/20-overview outrank an inline style= attribute
written by 29_ports.js / ui.js — no cascade layer can do that, only an author
!important can. theme/45-misc is the same case: the realtime graphs write
style='width:100%' on the box they draw into, and the theme has to widen it or
the drawing (sized from #view) is clipped by the card's gutter. theme/65-dropdown
carries the widget's three ul-margin flags (ui.js writes margin inline on an open
list), moved in with the machinery absorbed from base/80-dropdown.css. styles/base
keeps the .left/.right/.center forcing utilities and the two inline-style fighters
in 95-luci. theme/95-a11y-media is the reduced-motion block: !important INVERTS
the layer order, so it is the only way one rule can stop animations declared in
base as well as in theme.
This list is the contract. Adding a file to it needs a reason in the file.
The same list lives in tools/audit.py (BANG_OK); tools/bang-ok.mjs (npm run bang-ok)
fails if the two drift, so a file added here but not there (or vice versa) is caught.
